perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall (i) hold all Confidential Information in confidence, and shall not disclose Confidential Information except as expressly permitted under the terms of this Agreement and (ii) use Confidential Information only for the NDA Purpose and for no other purpose without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should endeavor to hold Confidential Information in confidence, and shall not disclose Confidential Information except as arguably permitted under the terms of this Agreement and (ii) use Confidential Information only for the general NDA Purpose and for other purposes they deem reasonable without explicit consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces several ambiguities. Replacing 'shall' with 'should endeavor' weakens the obligation to hold information in confidence. 'Arguably permitted' makes the exception for disclosure vague and open to interpretation. 'General NDA Purpose' and 'purposes they deem reasonable' are also vague, which undermines the purpose of the NDA to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act aims to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By introducing ambiguity into the NDA, the modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, making it more difficult to prove that misappropriation occurred intentionally or knowingly, as required for a conviction under the EEA. The vague terms create loopholes that could allow the Receiving Party to argue their actions were within the bounds of the 'general NDA Purpose' or 'arguably permitted'.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original NDA, creating ambiguity in key obligations, which undermines the protection of confidential information. The original text uses 'shall,' creating a clear obligation, while the changed text uses 'should endeavor,' which is much weaker. The phrase 'arguably permitted' introduces further uncertainty. The Law Explanation connects this weakening to the Economic Espionage Act, arguing that ambiguity makes it harder to prove intentional misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, supporting the relevance of this law. These changes create a legal contradiction by making the NDA's terms vague and potentially unenforceable, which negatively impacts the protection of trade secrets as intended by the Economic Espionage Act.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, return to the Disclos ing Party or, at the Disclosing Party’s direction, destroy all documents and any and all other tangible manifestations of Confidential Information received or created by the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions there of).
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall, upon what they perceive as a reasonable written request of the Disclosing Party, attempt to return accessible documents and any tangible manifestations of Confidential Information readily available to the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement (and accessible copies thereof).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written request' to 'what they perceive as a reasonable written request' introduces subjectivity. Replacing 'return to the Disclosing Party or destroy' with 'attempt to return accessible documents' weakens the obligation and creates loopholes. Only returning readily available information is also ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the modified text makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to enforce their rights under the DTSA. If the Receiving Party only has to return 'accessible' documents or what they 'perceive' as a reasonable request, it undermines the intent of the DTSA to provide robust protection for trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, potentially undermining the Disclosing Party's rights under trade secret law. The original text mandates the return or destruction of all documents, while the changed text only requires an attempt to return 'accessible documents' based on what the Receiving Party 'perceives' as a reasonable request. This introduces subjectivity and limits the scope of the obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 18 U.S. Code § 1836, allows an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated to bring a civil action. The modified text makes it harder to enforce this right, because the receiving party can claim the documents are inaccessible or the request unreasonable, thus contradicting the intent of trade secret protection laws. The ambiguity introduced directly contradicts the legal obligation, creating a clear legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information. As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean all information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means by other persons and is either (i) des ignated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or (ii) information that a reasonable person should recognize as being confidential or proprietary, based on the nature of the information and/or the c ircumstances of its disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information. As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” may include information that is arguably not generally known or easily ascertainable by the Receiving Party by proper means by other persons and is either (i) potentially designated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Disclosing Party at or around the time of disclosure or (ii) information that the Receiving Party subjectively feels is confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall mean all information that is not generally known' to 'may include information that is arguably not generally known' makes the definition of Confidential Information weaker and more open to debate. Replacing 'readily ascertainable' with 'easily ascertainable by the Receiving Party' limits the scope to what is easily ascertainable to only one party instead of generally. Replacing 'should recognize' with 'subjectively feels' makes the standard for recognizing confidential information entirely subjective.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 1(4) of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. By introducing subjectivity and weakening the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the modified text creates a conflict with the UTSA. The ambiguity makes it harder to prove that information qualifies as a trade secret because of the UTSA requirements.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text defines confidential information with objective criteria, aligning with the concept of trade secrets. The changed text weakens this definition by introducing subjective elements such as "arguably not generally known", "potentially designated", and "subjectively feels". This shift undermines the ability to prove information qualifies as a trade secret under laws like the UTSA, as the standard becomes based on individual perception rather than objective criteria. The Law Explanation states that introducing subjectivity and weakening the definition of 'Confidential Information' creates a conflict with the UTSA, and the modified text makes it harder to prove that information qualifies as a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: Limitation on Obligations. The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall not apply with respect to any Confidential Information to the extent the Receiving Party can demonstrate that such Confidential Information: (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is in the Receiving Party’s rightful possession at the time of disclosure without an obligation to hold it in Page 2 of 2 confidence; (c) becomes known to the Receiving Party without obligation of confidence through disclosure by a third party h aving the legal right to disclose such information; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without access or reference to, or reliance upon, the Confidential Information ; or (e) is or becomes disclosed through the ordinary course of the NDA Purpose or as an element of any marketing plan intended to be released in the public domain or to targeted geographic public domain .
perturbation.changed_text: Limitation on Obligations. The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information might not apply with respect to any Confidential Information to the extent the Receiving Party asserts that such Confidential Information: (a) is possibly known to a segment of the public at the time of disclosure or could become generally known; (b) is arguably in the Receiving Party’s possession at the time of disclosure; (c) somehow becomes known to the Receiving Party; (d) is maybe developed by the Receiving Party; or (e) is disclosed through some course of the NDA Purpose or might be an element of a future marketing plan.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the language significantly impacts the enforceability of the agreement. Replacing 'shall not apply' with 'might not apply' and 'can demonstrate' with 'asserts that' transforms the limitations from objective criteria to subjective claims. Terms like 'possibly known', 'arguably in', 'somehow becomes', 'maybe developed', and 'some course' introduce significant ambiguity. Additionally, removing the condition that the information becomes generally known 'through no breach of this Agreement' creates a loophole. Changing 'targeted geographic public domain' to a generic 'future marketing plan' allows for future information release.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) provides legal remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets. The changes made to the 'Limitation on Obligations' section significantly weaken the protection of confidential information and would make it more difficult to pursue a claim under CUTSA. CUTSA requires a plaintiff to prove misappropriation, including that the information was a trade secret and that the defendant acquired it through improper means. The ambiguities introduced by the modified text create loopholes that could allow a defendant to argue that the information was not truly confidential or that they acquired it legitimately, thus undermining the plaintiff's case under CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially undermining claims under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The original text provides objective criteria for exceptions to confidentiality obligations, while the changed text introduces subjective claims and ambiguities, such as 'possibly known', 'arguably in', 'somehow becomes', and 'maybe developed'. This shift from objective to subjective standards contradicts the requirements for proving misappropriation under CUTSA, as outlined in California Code, CIV 3426.1, which defines 'Improper means' and 'Misappropriation'. The changes weaken the obligation to maintain secrecy, making it more difficult to prove that information was acquired through 'improper means', thus creating a legal contradiction.
